text:
I work on different problem, so my framing is also different.

For me the first step into the future is in creating a structured language (text as UI allowed, but not as storage) with superpowers that aren't possible in textual languages. Make it take all possible advantages from its structured nature.

Superpowers should attract enough people to turn it into living thing. Only after that really start to invest into UI.

> I want individuals, not massive organizations to be doing the eating.
>
> PP: Wow. I guess that makes sense… But it’s also nice to have things uniform, such as a back button in the same place in all my apps, and all my connected Google services.
>
> Me: That’s a good point.

If an app consists of many isolated layers, then you can customize one layer, while others will sync with upstream code.

For me the first step into the future is in creating a structured language (text as UI allowed, but not as storage) with superpowers that aren't possible in textual languages. Make it take all possible advantages from its structured nature.

Superpowers should attract enough people to turn it into living thing. Only after that really start to invest into UI.

> I want individuals, not massive organizations to be doing the eating.
>
> PP: Wow. I guess that makes sense… But it’s also nice to have things uniform, such as a back button in the same place in all my apps, and all my connected Google services.
>
> Me: That’s a good point.

If an app consists of many isolated layers, then you can customize one layer, while others will sync with upstream code.

That sounds like it might be exactly my vision, modulo some clarifications.

For one, do you imagine Gmail adopting your approach to programming, if it existed?

That sounds like it might be exactly my vision, modulo some clarifications.

For one, do you imagine Gmail adopting your approach to programming, if it existed?

One conclusion I reluctantly arrived at after taking to lots of people: sometimes a vision being too broad can make it unpersuasive. Most laypeople are just not used to thinking so big. And the farther a programmer is from a sub-field, the harder it is to care about huge upheavals in it.

If you're working on a piece of a piece, maybe focus on it and explain how it may be useful without/before the rest.

(Because if it isn't useful by itself, maybe you aren't working on the right sub-problem.)

(Because if it isn't useful by itself, maybe you aren't working on the right sub-problem.)

Glad we're mostly on the same page <@UCUSW7WVD>! My new framing was definitely inspired by some of your writings. I think my sub problem is useful by itself but not to people at parties. Basically any technical subproblem would fit that description. That's why I feel like I need to zoom out a bit. However, I agree with you on being more specific. Maybe I simply say I'm building a fully customizable email app (and keep quiet on the fact that you'll be able to build any web app with it)

Glad we're mostly on the same page <@UCUSW7WVD>! My new framing was definitely inspired by some of your writings. I think my sub problem is useful by itself but not to people at parties. Basically any technical subproblem would fit that description. That's why I feel like I need to zoom out a bit. However, I agree with you on being more specific. Maybe I simply say I'm building a fully customizable email app (and keep quiet on the fact that you'll be able to build any web app with it)

Exactly. I think this is widely applicable advice: _"Better to start with an overly narrow description of your project than try to describe it in its full generality and lose the audience completely."_ -- <http://www.ycombinator.com/howtoapply>

There's also the question of tailoring your description to the specific party you're at. But we all wrestle with that..

How would you answer my Gmail question? To me there's no way to get someone like Gmail to adopt an entire new approach that reduces their power over users without first showing an alternative and demonstrating adoption. Show people a new thing that's more desirable than the old thing.

(Also, app stores are particularly challenging; you can't really build a powerful programmable app atop either Android or iOS. Thinking about mobile is inevitably depressing to me. If you have thoughts on how to use existing app stores I'm all ears.)

How would you answer my Gmail question? To me there's no way to get someone like Gmail to adopt an entire new approach that reduces their power over users without first showing an alternative and demonstrating adoption. Show people a new thing that's more desirable than the old thing.

(Also, app stores are particularly challenging; you can't really build a powerful programmable app atop either Android or iOS. Thinking about mobile is inevitably depressing to me. If you have thoughts on how to use existing app stores I'm all ears.)
Ah sorry I missed that question. My approach would be to build a competitor to gmail (potentially built as a "front end" to Gmail as you could login with that account and we'd forward to it)
Ah sorry I missed that question. My approach would be to build a competitor to gmail (potentially built as a "front end" to Gmail as you could login with that account and we'd forward to it)

As far as getting around app stores, I think the ideal world would be everything be web based, which I imagine is just a matter of time. Processors get faster, web standards include more of the hardware, etc. I'm not an expert on mobile development so I don't know how realistic that is but it's my hope

Hey <@U5TCAFTD3>, I like it very much, especially the optimistic outlook at the end. The only thing I didn't quite get was "getting rid of the settings menu". Even if it would be a lot easier to change existing apps, having a dedicated place for the most-used settings could still be useful in my opinion. I think settings are good, the problem is just that it's almost impossible to change anything not found in the settings.

Yes this is a great point. The spirit of my comment was: wouldnt it be neat if changing an app was so easy that we wouldn't need a settings menu because you could just change the value of a constant somewhere? But I totally see your point that a settings menu may never be beatable for simplicity

Consider that even if Gmail's source was trivial to change, doing so without someway of deploying your changes wouldn't be of much value.  Software isn't just it's code, it's how it's embedded, connected to the rest of the world.

Consider that even if Gmail's source was trivial to change, doing so without someway of deploying your changes wouldn't be of much value.  Software isn't just it's code, it's how it's embedded, connected to the rest of the world.

I agree with <@UCUSW7WVD> on narrow descriptions.  The problem with a general tool is seeing how the specific problem at hand fits into the general context.  Think of how often you have Googled how to do something specific at the shell knowing for sure that a clever piping of three commands is the solution you're looking for.

<@UAL7940NM> though strictly right about the settings menu, it's the sentiment, the sense of "where we're going we don't need roads" or "I’m trying to tell you that when you’re ready, you won’t have to."

Totally agreed on your first point <@UA14TGLTC>. @r0ml calls this "liberal software" when you have the freedom to deploy your changes to a forked runtime. I agree it's essential to this vision. There should be no "getting your code to run on your own machine" step

Locality should be somewhat incidental, like a good note taking app: online/offline is a thing but it mostly works.
Locality should be somewhat incidental, like a good note taking app: online/offline is a thing but it mostly works.

I once wrote a Chrome extension to modify the Reddit UI in a small way: it would highlight any comment that had a higher score than its parent comment. This is a great heuristic for finding excellent comments.

It took me like a day to build. After a few months it broke -- I assume Reddit must’ve updated their UI some small way which broke my comment parsing. I never took the time to fix it.

I’m not sure what the moral of this story is.

attachments.title: Is It Worth the Time?
attachments.text: [Title text] "Don't forget the time you spend finding the chart to look up what you save. And the time spent reading this reminder about the time spent. And the time trying to figure out if either of those actually make sense. Remember, every second counts toward your life total, including these right now."
attachments.fallback: xkcd: Is It Worth the Time?

text: <@U5TCAFTD3>, by any chance have you seen <http://www.hyperfiddle.net/>? I’m curious as to how it is similar/different from what you want to accomplish =)…
<@U5TCAFTD3>, by any chance have you seen <http://www.hyperfiddle.net/>? I’m curious as to how it is similar/different from what you want to accomplish =)…

Thanks <@UDPV407CZ>! This is a very cool platform I hadn't seen. Where did you find it?

It definitely is similar to what I'm looking to accomplish in many ways: it blurs the distinction between front and backend, allows for quick prototyping.

However I don't think it is trying to accomplish my goal of "allowing people who use software to edit that software in the course of using it". That is, it doesn't seem to create apps that are modifiable by their users. (Their model does seem quite simple so it would be easier for users to edit if they were given permissions.)

Additionally, I believe that often what people want to edit most about an app is its user interface, which is definitely not a focus here. My work is more focused on the user interface at the moment, and eventually I'll want to extend to the backend...

Does that answer your question?

Ok, just for some background in case it’s useful, I’m building a complex data visualiser for business/non-tech people. My friend who’s working on it with me is a business guy, so I regularly have to work out how to make it more accessible =)… We’ve got some users and I’m really very interested in making computation more accessible.
